title: A Heterogeneous CNN Compilation Framework for RISC-V CPU and NPU Integration Based on ONNX-MLIR
abstract: With the continuous advancement of convolutional neural networks (CNNs), many neural network processing units (NPUs) have emerged in recent years. NPUs offer advantages, such as improved energy efficiency and low latency compared to traditional processors. However, NPUs often struggle to keep pace with the growing complexity of algorithmic models, which limits the implementation of certain AI applications. Central processing units (CPUs) are known for their versatility, but are computationally inefficient. Combining the strengths of both architectures could present a viable solution to these challenges. Despite this potential, there is currently insufficient academic focus on CPU/NPU co-computing, and few architectures or toolchains have been developed to support such integration. In this article, we propose a RISC-V CPU/NPU co-computing-based compilation framework to solve the problem of mapping from algorithms to heterogeneous architectures. To bridge the computational gap between heterogeneous platforms, within ONNX-MLIR, we developed three phases to support operator packing, algorithm adaption, and data coordination. To integrate heterogeneous compilation platforms, we introduce four additional functions to handle task scheduling, weight quantization, weight rearrangement, and memory management. In particular: 1) to optimize the transition overhead, an operator packing technique is introduced by extending the ONNX-MLIR framework with custom operators, which enhances NPU efficiency by reducing the number of transitions between operations; 2) at the aspect of task scheduling, a three-mode task scheduler is developed to allow users to customize task distribution for correctness verification, performance optimization, or detailed analysis on heterogeneous platforms, which demonstrates the versatility of our framework; and 3) In terms of memory management, an optimized scheme for CPU/NPU architectures is proposed to ensure accurate data access. This memory management employs a dual-end approach with self-checks and a release-after-use mechanism to further improve efficiency. Experimental results demonstrate that the framework effectively generates instructions for various CNNs, significantly enhancing the efficiency and performance of heterogeneous architectures. It achieves up to a $7.06\times $ reduction in code density and up to a $5.58\times $ improvement in memory usage, narrowing the computational gap between CPUs and NPUs.
body_text:
IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMPUTER-AIDED DESIGN OF INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SYSTEMS, VOL. 44, NO. 11, NOVEMBER 2025
A Heterogeneous CNN Compilation Framework
for RISC-V CPU and NPU Integration Based
on ONNX-MLIR
Shan Cao
, Member, IEEE, Meiling Yang, Yintao Liu, Yu Li,
Beining Zhao, Graduate Student Member, IEEE, Xinyu Chen, and Zhiyuan Jiang
, Member, IEEE
Abstract—With the continuous advancement of convolutional
neural networks (CNNs), many neural network processing units
(NPUs) have emerged in recent years. NPUs offer advantages,
such as improved energy efﬁciency and low latency compared to
traditional processors. However, NPUs often struggle to keep pace
with the growing complexity of algorithmic models, which limits
the implementation of certain AI applications. Central processing
units (CPUs) are known for their versatility, but are computa-
tionally inefﬁcient. Combining the strengths of both architectures
could present a viable solution to these challenges. Despite
this potential, there is currently insufﬁcient academic focus on
CPU/NPU co-computing, and few architectures or toolchains
have been developed to support such integration. In this article,
we propose a RISC-V CPU/NPU co-computing-based compilation
framework to solve the problem of mapping from algorithms
to heterogeneous architectures. To bridge the computational
gap between heterogeneous platforms, within ONNX-MLIR, we
developed three phases to support operator packing, algorithm
adaption, and data coordination. To integrate heterogeneous
compilation platforms, we introduce four additional functions
to handle task scheduling, weight quantization, weight rear-
rangement, and memory management. In particular: 1) to
optimize the transition overhead, an operator packing technique
is introduced by extending the ONNX-MLIR framework with
custom operators, which enhances NPU efﬁciency by reducing
the number of transitions between operations; 2) at the aspect
of task scheduling, a three-mode task scheduler is developed
to allow users to customize task distribution for correctness
veriﬁcation, performance optimization, or detailed analysis on
heterogeneous platforms, which demonstrates the versatility of
our framework; and 3) In terms of memory management, an
optimized scheme for CPU/NPU architectures is proposed to
ensure accurate data access. This memory management employs
a dual-end approach with self-checks and a release-after-use
mechanism to further improve efﬁciency. Experimental results
demonstrate that the framework effectively generates instructions
for various CNNs, signiﬁcantly enhancing the efﬁciency and
performance of heterogeneous architectures. It achieves up to a

7.06× reduction in code density and up to a 5.58× improvement
in memory usage, narrowing the computational gap between
CPUs and NPUs.
Index Terms—Compilation framework, heterogeneous plat-
form, neural processing unit (NPU), ONNX-MLIR, RISC-V.
I. INTRODUCTION
C
ONVOLUTIONAL neural networks (CNNs) have shown
remarkable advantages in image processing and are
extensively used in various artiﬁcial intelligence (AI) appli-
cations, such as face recognition [1], object detection [2],
and automatic driving [3]. However, as the scale and appli-
cation scenario of these networks increases, CNNs introduce
higher computation capability and energy efﬁciency demands,
exceeding the capabilities of central processing units (CPUs)
and graphics processing units (GPUs). To address this growing
need, the design of efﬁcient processors speciﬁcally tailored for
neural networks, known as neural processing units (NPUs),
has become essential.
NPUs play a crucial role in the advancement of AI, offering
beneﬁts, such as low energy consumption, low latency, high
computing efﬁciency, and programmability. However, NPU
tends to be less generalizable due to design cost consider-
ations. They also struggle to keep pace with the increasing
complexity of algorithmic models, limiting the implementa-
tion of CNNs with diverse operators. Although CPUs are
known for their versatility, they suffer from low energy
efﬁciency for data- and computation-intensive CNN inference
tasks [4]. Therefore, a heterogeneous CPU/NPU platform is
essential to perform multiple CNN inference computations
while meeting performance and power constraints. However,
there is currently a lack of academic focus on CPU/NPU
research, and few architectures or toolchains have been
developed to support co-computing between these two types of
processors.
On the CPU side, the RISC-V architecture is gaining pop-
ularity in edge computing due to its open-source and modular
nature. It allows ﬂexible selection of instruction subsets tai-
lored to speciﬁc applications. For example, RV32IMC is used
in [5] for low-power scenarios in small areas. Additionally,
RISC-V reserves signiﬁcant instruction code space for user-
deﬁned extensions [6], [7], making it ideal for domain-speciﬁc
enhancements. A notable example is RV-CNN, a domain-
speciﬁc instruction set with nine matrix instructions based
on the custom’ instruction in [7], which improves code
1937-4151 c⃝2025 IEEE. All rights reserved, including rights for text and data mining, and training of artiﬁcial intelligence

See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information.
CAO et al.: HETEROGENEOUS CNN COMPILATION FRAMEWORK
density while maintaining ﬂexibility and efﬁciency for CNNs.
Considerable academic work [8], [9] has explored mapping
CNNs to RISC-V instructions, but most efforts focus on neural
network deployment rather than computational efﬁciency.
The ONNX-MLIR toolchain [10] is a modular, open-source
framework that supports adaptive scaling, converting ONNX
input into multilevel intermediate representations (MLIRs).
However, it currently lacks support for generating back-end
instructions for heterogeneous architectures.
On the NPU side, customization requirements necessitate
a dedicated CNN compiler for generating instructions. The
compilers of CNN accelerators serve as a bridge between hard-
ware and algorithms, optimizing network models based on the
target hardware architecture. Compilation-level optimization
techniques, such as loop tiling [11], can help address the
limitations of network deployment imposed by hardware
resource constraints. However, challenges remain, including
unimplemented operators and unique architectures. These
issues highlight the need for a heterogeneous computing
architecture that can integrate NPUs with other processing
units, enabling cross-architecture optimization and leveraging
the strengths of diverse hardware platforms.
Different hardware architectures often have their own
unique instruction sets and capabilities. To integrate a
heterogeneous compilation platform that supports multiple
architectures, it is essential to ensure that the generated code
is compatible across all targeted devices. Many architec-
tures [12], [13] designed for heterogeneous computing have
emerged in recent years. Additionally, several architectures
that exhibit heterogeneous-like characteristics have been intro-
duced [14], [15]. However, these architectures often support
only a limited number of CNNs and uses the CPU solely as
a host, without incorporating it into the computation process.
This limitation restricts the potential for extending generality
in these systems.
To address the aforementioned issues, this article presents
a versatile compilation framework built upon the RISC-V
CPU and NPU co-computing architecture. This framework
is designed to efﬁciently map algorithms to heterogeneous
architectures. As illustrated in Fig. 1, it accepts a pruned
and quantized ONNX format ﬁle as input, and integrates a
custom CNN compiler with the open-source ONNX-MLIR
toolchain. Following this, decision-making processes and
various optimization techniques are applied to generate the
corresponding RISC-V instruction set architecture (ISA) and
NPU ISA for implementation on the heterogeneous archi-
tecture. The main contributions of this work include the
following.
1) By extending the ONNX-MLIR framework with custom
operators, we introduce an operator packing technique
that maximizes the computational efﬁciency of NPUs,
while minimizing overhead during transitions between
heterogeneous operations.
2) A task scheduler with three operational modes is
developed, allowing users to customize the task dis-
tribution according to speciﬁc requirements, including
correctness veriﬁcation, performance optimization, or
detailed analysis of computational workﬂows across
Fig. 1.
Illustration of the heterogeneous compilation framework and its
interfaces toward external tools, with details on the components extended for
the generation of heterogeneous instructions.
heterogeneous platforms, which typically emphasizes
the versatility of the framework.
3) The discrete memory allocation strategy is optimized to
accommodate the CPU/NPU heterogeneous architecture,
ensuring seamless data access during cross-platform
computations. To further enhance memory efﬁciency,
the memory management system incorporates two tech-
niques: a dual-end (DE) allocation strategy with a
self-check function, and a release-after-use (RU) mech-
anism that minimizes memory footprint.
The remainder of the article is structured as follows. The
related works are listed in Section II. In Section III, the
backgrounds of the framework are introduced; in Section IV,
the overview of our heterogeneous compilation framework
is presented. Within ONNX-MLIR optimizations, including
operator packing, algorithm adaption, and data coordination
are presented in Section V. Section VI illustrates four func-
tions to seamlessly integrate the heterogeneous compilation.
The thorough evaluation of the framework and technologies is
described and analyzed in Section VII, and the whole work is
summarized in Section VIII.
II. RELATED WORKS
The rise of heterogeneous computing introduces signiﬁ-
cant challenges in mapping workloads to diverse hardware
accelerators, particularly under resource-constrained environ-
ments. Previous works have explored various partitioning and
optimization strategies to efﬁciently allocate computations
across different processing units.
TVM [16] is an open-source compiler framework that
applies multilevel IR optimizations for deploying models on
IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMPUTER-AIDED DESIGN OF INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SYSTEMS, VOL. 44, NO. 11, NOVEMBER 2025
various hardware backends. Its abstraction layers, Relay and
TIR, enable hardware-speciﬁc code generation but do not
support cross-device collaboration. TensorRT, developed by
NVIDIA, is widely used for GPU inference and applies
graph-level optimizations, such as layer fusion and precision
calibration. However, like TVM, it mainly targets single-
accelerator deployment and lacks native support for CPU/NPU
cooperative execution.
Curzel et al. [14] introduced a compilation toolchain sim-
ilar to ONNX-MLIR but incorporate high-level synthesis
techniques on the backend to generate efﬁcient accelerators.
This approach provides an effective means of improving
hardware efﬁciency by optimizing computation at the design
stage. Liu et al. [13] presented a CPU/FPGA/NPU hetero-
geneous system for UAV applications, utilizing the uniﬁed
programming language DPC++ [17] to streamline develop-
ment across diverse computing platforms. This demonstrates
the efﬁciency of heterogeneous architectures in specialized
scenarios. Similarly, Qin et al. [12] introduced a CNN
hardware accelerator within a RISC-V-based heterogeneous
system, speciﬁcally designed for YOLOv3-Tiny. Their work
showcases a well-optimized hardware-software co-design for
deep learning inference. NEST-C [18] is a deep learning
compiler framework that enhances model deployment and
performance across AI accelerators. It employs proﬁling-based
quantization, dynamic graph partitioning, and multilevel IR
integration for efﬁcient execution on heterogeneous hardware.
Additionally, it features PartitionTuner [19], an operator sched-
uler that optimally distributes workloads across CPUs, NPUs,
and other processing units to minimize inference time.
Overall,
these
works
contribute
signiﬁcantly
to
the
ﬁeld of heterogeneous computing by introducing effective
optimization strategies, compilation techniques, and hardware-
software co-design methods. Building on these efforts, our
work focuses on a ﬂexible and generalizable framework
that leverages ONNX-MLIR’s multilevel dialect structure for
efﬁcient task partitioning and resource management, enabling
broader applicability across various CNN models and hetero-
geneous computing scenarios.
III. PRELIMINARIES
Mapping CNNs to heterogeneous architectures involves
leveraging two key techniques. First, ONNX-MLIR generates
valid instructions for CNN operators on RISC-V and produces
intermediate representations at multiple levels, which enhances
the ability to manage various tasks effectively. Second, the
RISC-V instruction extension plays a crucial role in enabling
the integration of heterogeneous instructions.
A. ONNX-MLIR Toolchain
ONNX is a widely used standard that enables seamless AI
model transfer between frameworks like PyTorch [20] and
TensorFlow [21], facilitating efﬁcient transitions from research
to production. Before deployment, models often undergo
optimization techniques, such as pruning [22] and quanti-
zation [23]. Researchers apply these optimizations across
different frameworks and export the reﬁned models in ONNX
format for implementation. Additionally, ONNX ﬁles are
highly readable and accessible via the “protoc” command or
visualization tools like “netron.”
MLIR [24] is an extensible IR supporting various abstrac-
tion
levels,
from
high-level
algorithmic
descriptions
to
low-level instruction sets. It provides tools for static type
checking, code veriﬁcation, and optimization, ensuring efﬁ-
cient target code generation. The ONNX-MLIR project
includes three dialect levels: 1) ONNX; 2) MLIR; and
3) LLVM. With its mature I/O interfaces and ﬂexible IRs,
we use ONNX-MLIR as the foundation of our heterogeneous
compilation framework for mapping CNNs to RISC-V instruc-
tions.
An ONNX ﬁle can be converted into the ONNX dialect,
which offers high readability and aids in heterogeneous task
decisions. It separates constant inputs for each network layer,
facilitating parameter organization and optimization. Each
row represents a network layer, containing all necessary
computation parameters, which are useful for both instruction
generation and task assignment between CPU and NPU. The
MLIR dialect expresses operations as loop-based computa-
tions, enabling optimizations like tiling and unrolling [25].
Finally, the LLVM dialect handles memory allocation and
utilizes low-level “llvm” functions, making it well-suited for
address reassignment in heterogeneous architectures.
B. RISC-V Instruction Extension
In the standard encoding format of RISC-V, there are
three “reserved” and four “custom” opcodes designated for
instruction extension, all preﬁxed with “11” (in bit 1:0 of
the instruction), effectively offering a signiﬁcant amount of
encoding space. Additionally, there are three 30-bit instruction
encoding spaces, preﬁxed with “00,” “01,” and “10,” available
for optional compressed instruction extensions. However, if
the compressed instruction set extension is not required, these
three 30-bit encoding spaces can be repurposed for uses.
For instance, the “00” preﬁx could be used for a set of
custom vector operations to support enhanced functionality
without expanding beyond the 32-bit format. Similarly, the
“01” preﬁx could be used for a different class of extended
instructions, such as specialized arithmetic operations. This
approach maximizes the encoding capacity of the RISC-
V architecture, providing ﬂexibility for advanced instruction
extensions without the immediate need for the compressed
instruction sets.
IV. OVERVIEW OF COMPILATION FRAMEWORK
A. Overview
Fig. 2(a) provides the overview of our heterogeneous
compilation framework. To bridge the computational gap
between heterogeneous platforms, we developed three phases
within ONNX-MLIR:
1) operator packing; 2) algorithm
adaptation; and 3) data coordination. Additionally, we intro-
duce four functions to integrate heterogeneous compilation
platforms, including task scheduling, weight quantization,
weight rearrangement, and memory management. Importantly,
our framework considers hardware resource constraints and
CAO et al.: HETEROGENEOUS CNN COMPILATION FRAMEWORK
(a)
(b)
(c)
Fig. 2.
Overview of heterogeneous compilation framework. (a) Workﬂow of our compilation framework. (b) Workﬂow of our previous NPU compiler,
including translation, optimization, and execution. (c) Illustration of the MLIR.
TABLE I
HARDWARE AND SOFTWARE CONFIGURATION INPUT SUPPORTED
BY THE COMPILATION FRAMEWORK
includes dynamic quantization at the software level, as detailed
in Table I, which allows for high conﬁgurability.
The task scheduler takes the ONNX dialect as input,
partitions tasks between the CPU and NPU based on hardware
constraints or other speciﬁc requirements. It then relays the
partitioning results back to ONNX-MLIR for operator packing.
At the same time, the task scheduler outputs the NPU operators
to the NPU compiler, enabling the mapping of these operators
to NPU instructions.
Operator packing involves grouping the partitioned oper-
ators into CPU and NPU functions, so that the instructions
in the NPU functions can be subsequently replaced by
the corresponding NPU instructions. The NPU is not only
constrained by the types of operators it can compute but
also by the speciﬁc parameter limitations of each operator.
For example, many NPU architectures feature convolution
operations with standard feature map (FM) sizes, which is
not required for CPUs. This difference necessitates algo-
rithm adaptation between the two platforms. This approach
not only streamlines the data ﬂow between the CPU and
NPU, but also facilitates more efﬁcient utilization of the
NPU’s specialized capabilities. Although this adaptation intro-
duces some computational overhead on the CPU side, it
will be shown that the beneﬁts far outweigh this minor
cost.
The weight quantizer converts ﬂoating-point constants from
the NPU in the MLIR dialect into ﬁxed-point numbers based
on dynamic quantization parameters. The quantized data are
then combined with the layer parameters generated during the
optimization phase of the CNN compiler to perform weight
rearrangement, tailored to the hardware architecture. Finally,
the data is returned to the MLIR dialect and stored as “NPU
constants,” allowing us to retrieve the constant addresses for
each layer during the generation phase.
Moreover, performance considerations for the NPU archi-
tecture result in differences in how FM data is stored between
the NPU and CPU. To address this, data coordination is inte-
grated within the MLIR dialect. Once this data is passed to the
LLVM dialect, the memory manager uniﬁes the management
of CPU and NPU data spaces, ensuring the correctness of data
access across layers while optimizing memory usage.
To ensure compatibility within a heterogeneous archi-
tecture, each 512-bit NPU instruction must be split into
18 individual 32-bit instructions in the RISC-V format. In
this conﬁguration, the “00” preﬁx is assigned to NPU-
speciﬁc instructions, while the “10” preﬁx indicates the ﬁnal
instruction in the sequence. This transformation enables the
seamless integration of NPU instructions within the RISC-V
architecture.
B. NPU Compiler
The NPU compiler in the heterrogeneous compiliation
framework is based on our previous work [11], designed for
the NPU in [26]. The compilation process consists of three
IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMPUTER-AIDED DESIGN OF INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SYSTEMS, VOL. 44, NO. 11, NOVEMBER 2025
phases: 1) translation; 2) optimization; and 3) generation, as
shown in Fig. 2(b).
In the translation phase, network layers are analyzed via
text matching and categorized into ﬁve operation types:
1) convolution; 2) fully connected (FC) layers; 3) pooling;
4) concatenation; and 5) element addition. The model is then
converted into an intermediate format [Format 2 in Fig. 2(c)],
containing only essential parameters, such as operation types,
layer details, and special layer identiﬁers.
During optimization, the network model is reorganized
through techniques like layer fusion and splitting to better
ﬁt the accelerator. A compiler-level loop tiling method is
introduced to divide large layers into smaller ones, reducing
storage and memory access demands. Additionally, weight and
bias data are reordered alongside layer splitting.
In the generation phase, the ﬁnal CNN representation, along
with weight and bias data, is produced for the accelerator.
Data is reordered per the accelerator’s requirements [27], and
additional parameters are computed. PE mask bits are also
deployed to support padding, stride, and dilation operations,
enhancing accelerator versatility.
C. GUI for Compilation Framework
To fully emphasize the versatility of our framework, we
have developed a graphical user interface (GUI) to enhance
user experience and meet on-demand needs. At the algorithmic
level, our compilation framework supports a wide range
of CNNs, including the Resnet series, VGG [28] series,
MobileNet [29] series, and more. Additionally, users can fully
customize all hardware conﬁgurations, as outlined in Table I.
Especially for quantization format, our architecture supports
a variety of data types for different backends. Depending on
the application scenario, users can specify whether the CPU
should compute in ﬂoating point or use the same data type as
the NPU. Furthermore, users can input the optimal ﬁxed-point
parameters generated by the upper-level quantization platform
through user-deﬁned conﬁgurations.
Beyond the default task division strategy, where all NPU-
implementable operators are designated as NPU tasks, users
can opt for all-CPU computation to verify the correctness of
NPU operations. In addition to this, users can manually specify
the division of tasks by entering a set of binary numbers: “0”
for CPU tasks and “1” for NPU tasks, making it easy to com-
pare the acceleration effects of different hardware platforms.
This functionality can also be achieved by modifying the
“implementable operators,” which simpliﬁes the exploration of
acceleration effects for speciﬁc classes of operators, including
the associated constraints.
V. OPTIMIZATION WITHIN ONNX-MLIR
Based on the architecture in Fig. 2, we have developed three
phases within ONNX-MLIR to optimize dialects, enhancing
the convergence of heterogeneous platforms.
A. Operator Packing
A key technique for effectively leveraging the capabilities of
NPUs is CNN operator packing within the MLIR framework.
In the MLIR ecosystem, CNN operator packing involves
encapsulating multiple operations into composite ones, which
can be mapped more efﬁciently to NPU instructions. The
primary goal is to streamline the execution of CNN models by
replacing these packed operators with highly optimized NPU
instructions during inference. This approach reduces the over-
head associated with individual operations, and maximizes the
parallelism and specialized capabilities of NPUs, signiﬁcantly
accelerating inference times.
1) Adding Custom Operators to the Framework: To ensure
effective instruction substitutions, our custom operators should
be added to the ONNX-MLIR toolchain before completing the
operator packing. This process necessitates careful considera-
tion of the target dialects and their interaction [10].
The ﬁrst step in extending ONNX-MLIR is to deﬁne new
operators within the ONNX dialect. This process involves
specifying the name, inputs, outputs, and attributes of the oper-
ator according to the existing ONNX schema. The operator
must then be registered with the ONNX dialect to ensure it
is recognized and correctly parsed by the compiler. Once the
operator is deﬁned in the ONNX dialect, the next step is to
lower it to the MLIR dialect. It involves transforming the high-
level operation into a form that can be optimized. Finally,
the operator is lowered to the LLVM dialect and the newly
added operator should be compatible with the LLVM backend,
ensuring that it can be compiled into efﬁcient machine code
for the target architecture.
In the proposed heterogeneous compilation framework, the
instruction generation for the NPU takes place outside of
the ONNX-MLIR toolchain. As a result, the packed operator
does not need to characterize either the operator information
or the parameter information within the operator. However,
it is important to ensure that the input and output variable
information is preserved to prevent optimization during dialect
transfer. Therefore, following the steps for adding new oper-
ators and considering our speciﬁc requirements, three new
operators are added to the ONNX dialect.
1) %L1
=
“onnx.I3O1OfNPU”(%L0,
%w1,
%b1)
func_name
=
@I3O1OfNPU0
:
(tensor<*>,
tensor<*>, tensor<*>) -> tensor<*>
2) %L2
=
“onnx.I4O1OfNPU”(%L1,
%w2,
%b2,
%L0) func_name = @I4O1OfNPU0 : (tensor<*>,
tensor<*>, tensor<*>, tensor<*>) -> tensor<*>
3) %L3
=
“onnx.I1O1OfNPU”(%L2)
func_name
=
@I1O1OfNPU0 : (tensor<*>) -> tensor<*>.
As outlined above, we classify NPU operators into three
types based on input and output count, with their optimization
handled within the CNN compiler. In ONNX-MLIR, particu-
larly at the MLIR and LLVM dialect stages, the key task is to
accurately transmit the corresponding wrapper function names.
This enables correct recognition and use of the operators
during ﬁnal compilation, eliminating the need for further
optimization in these stages. Delegating optimization to the
CNN compiler simpliﬁes NPU integration and improves the
efﬁciency and scalability of the deployment pipeline.
2) Intermediate Steps in Operator Packing: Fig. 3 illus-
trates the intermediate steps involved in operator packing.
Taking
the
classic
MNIST
network
as
an
example,
CAO et al.: HETEROGENEOUS CNN COMPILATION FRAMEWORK
(a)
(b)
(c)
(d)
Fig. 3.
Intermediate steps in operator packing, mapping from CNN models to hardware. (a) Input computational graph of the MNIST network represented in
ONNX. (b) Lowering high-Level ONNX operations to the ONNX dialect in MLIR (simpliﬁed). (c) Format of the ONNX dialect after searching and operator
packing. (d) Final distribution of operations across the CPU and NPU.
Fig. 3(a) shows the input computational graph of the model
expressed in ONNX. This graph consists of a sequence of
operations, including Maxpool, Reshape, two instances of
general matrix multiplication (Gemm), ReLU, and Softmax.
Fig. 3(b) depicts the lowering process within the ONNX-
MLIR compiler, where the high-level ONNX operations are
mapped to the ONNX dialect in MLIR. In this intermediate
representation, the operations are expressed as function calls,
preserving the structure of the original computational graph.
This step serves as the initial phase of the compilation process,
preparing the operations for further optimization and eventual
translation to hardware-speciﬁc dialects.
After conducting a search and operator packing, certain
operations, such as Gemm and ReLU, have been consolidated
into NPU functions (e.g., NPU0 and NPU1), as illustrated in
Fig 3(c). Other operations, including Maxpool and Reshape,
have been assigned into CPU functions. Following the
downward mapping process, these operations are ultimately
distributed across various processing units, encompassing both
CPU and NPU, as illustrated in Fig 3(d). For instance, some
operations are executed on the CPU (e.g., CPU0 and CPU1),
while others are ofﬂoaded to the NPU. The system bus
facilitates communication between these processing units and
memory, ensuring efﬁcient data transfer and processing across
different hardware components.
3) Packing Strategy: For operations intended to be exe-
cuted on the CPU, adjacent operators are grouped together into
a single CPU function. This strategy minimizes the overhead
related to function calls and data transfers between opera-
tions, thereby enhancing computational efﬁciency [30]. By
consolidating CPU-bound operations, the framework ensures
more effective utilization of the resources on the CPU, which
reduces the latency and improves the overall performance.
For operations intended for execution on the NPU, operator
packing is to maximize the throughput of the NPU by aligning
the operations with its speciﬁc instructions. This packing strat-
egy takes into consideration the capacity of NPU to efﬁciently
handle composite operations. Three main combinations of
operators are considered for NPU packing.
1) CONV
+
(ReLU/LeakyReLU/MaxPool/Average-Pool/
Concat): By packing these operations together, the NPU
can process them in a streamlined manner, minimizing
the need for intermediate data transfers and reloading of
operands.
2) CONV
+
Add
+
(ReLU/LeakyReLU/MaxPool/
AveragePool/Concat): When a convolutional operation
is followed by an element-wise addition (Add) and
then an activation or pooling operation, combining
these steps into a single NPU function can signiﬁcantly
improve execution efﬁciency. This approach is especially
advantageous in layers that include residual connections,
such as in ResNet [31] architectures, where the addition
operations are commonly utilized.
3) GEMM + (ReLU/LeakyReLU/Reshape/Flatten): Similar
as Convolution, by grouping these operations, the NPU
can handle them as a single unit. This is essential
for maintaining high throughput in the dense layers of
neural networks.
This operator packing strategy optimizes the use of com-
putational resources across CPUs and NPUs while reducing
overhead during transitions between operations. By aligning
the packing process with the instruction-level execution of the
NPU, this approach maximizes hardware utilization, leading
to faster inference times and more efﬁcient execution of neural
networks on heterogeneous systems.
B. Algorithm Adaption
Despite their computational efﬁciency, NPUs often face
hardware-speciﬁc limitations regarding the types of operations
they can perform and the parameters they support. These
constraints necessitate careful consideration when adapting
algorithms for integration with more ﬂexible CPU-based
platforms.
First, on most NPUs, convolutional computations are opti-
mized for FMs sizes that are powers of two (e.g., 32 ×
16 × 16, 128 × 32 × 32). This limitation stems from the
internal architecture of NPU, which is designed to maximize
parallelism and memory access efﬁciency when handling data
IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMPUTER-AIDED DESIGN OF INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SYSTEMS, VOL. 44, NO. 11, NOVEMBER 2025
Fig. 4.
Data coordination in the MLIR dialect involves modifying the CPU’s FM storage order to match that of the NPU. This algorithm implements a
ﬁxed-point quantization of the input FM.
of speciﬁc sizes. In contrast, CPUs do not impose such
strict limitations on FM sizes, allowing for greater ﬂexibility
in executing convolutional operations. This discrepancy in
operational parameters between NPUs and CPUs presents
a signiﬁcant challenge when attempting to deploy neural
networks across heterogeneous platforms that incorporate both
types of processors.
One effective strategy to address this challenge is to use the
padding [32] and reshaping techniques to conform non-power-
of-2 FMs to NPU-compatible sizes. This process involves
padding the FMs with zeros or other values to expand their
dimensions to the nearest power of 2, thus enabling efﬁcient
processing on the NPU. Notably, padding is only required
in the row and channel dimensions. After the processing is
complete, any extraneous padding can be removed or adjusted
to restore the original dimensions of the FM before transferring
the data back to the CPU or subsequent layers in the neural
network. To minimize additional design overheads, we modify
CPU computations to align with NPU processing.
Second, heterogeneous platforms often face challenges with
inconsistent accuracy. Typically, CPUs use double-precision
(64-bit) ﬂoating-point arithmetic, whereas NPUs may utilize
reduced precision formats, such as 16-bit or even 8-bit integer
representations [33]. This shift to lower precision on NPUs
can lead to rounding errors and diminished accuracy in com-
putations, which may result in discrepancies when compared
to the results produced by CPUs.
At the compiler level, there are two primary approaches to
address this issue. First, we can introduce inverse ﬁxed-point
quantization operators before CPU functions and ﬁxed-point
quantization operators after them. This process allows CPU
operators to perform pseudo-ﬁxed-point computation, which
can enhance accuracy to some extent. Second, it is also
possible to convert all CPU operators directly to ﬁxed-
point computation, which improves computational efﬁciency.
However, certain operators in ONNX, such as Softmax, do
not support direct ﬁxed-point computation [34]. In these
cases, the ﬁrst method becomes necessary. In our compilation
framework, both methods are implemented, allowing users
the ﬂexibility to choose the approach that best suits their
application scenario.
C. Data Coordination
Aside from the possible differences in FM sizes, the order
in which the FMs are arranged may also vary. Typically, CPUs
employ row-major storage to optimize memory access patterns
and enhance cache utilization. Conversely, NPUs often use
optimized orders, such as channel-major or block-major to
enhance parallel processing efﬁciency [35]. This difference in
ordering requires data rearrangement when it is transferred
between the CPU and NPU, which increases the overhead
of memory accesses and introduces additional latency, which
ultimately impacts the overall performance.
Various strategies have been proposed to optimize FM
layouts on heterogeneous platforms, including automatic lay-
out transformation [16], hardware-aware neural architecture
search [36], custom compiler passes, and so on. Given the high
cost of altering hardware design, we have chosen to modify
the CPU’s FM storage order to align with that of the NPU.
The operation is translated into a loop-based computation in
the MLIR dialect, allowing for data coordination for CPU
operators, as shown in Fig. 4. Our NPU platform employs
the channel-major computation, in which the order of the
FMs is speciﬁed as [col, channel, row], requiring us to swap
the intra-CPU column dimension with the channel dimension.
Our method effectively allows for seamless data coordination
across heterogeneous platforms with minimal code changes
and negligible overhead, as illustrated in Fig. 4.
VI. INTEGRATION OF HETEROGENEOUS
COMPILATION PLATFORM
Heterogeneous compilation platforms vary in their inputs
and outputs, optimization methods, memory allocation, and
more. To integrate these platforms seamlessly, we developed
four functions that focus on task scheduling, weight quantizer,
weight reorderer, and memory management across both plat-
forms.
A. Task Scheduler
In the task scheduling strategy for heterogeneous systems,
three modes are provided, as outlined in Algorithm 1, to
manage the assignment of operations between the CPU and
the NPU according to the user-speciﬁc requirements.
1) All_CPU Mode: In certain situations, users may wish
to verify the correctness of the computations performed on the
NPU by comparing them with results obtained from the CPU.
To facilitate this, the algorithm offers an ALL_CPU mode,
which forces all tasks to be executed on the CPU, regardless of
their compatibility with the NPU. This approach guarantees a
consistent computational environment for accuracy validation.
CAO et al.: HETEROGENEOUS CNN COMPILATION FRAMEWORK
Algorithm 1 Task Scheduling of CPU/NPU Operators
Input: CM: calculation mode, OD: ONNX dialect, NO:
number of operators, (TD: task designation, NR: NPU
restrictions);
Output: TC: task of CPU, TN: task of NPU;
1: for each i ∈[1, NO] do
2:
if CM == ALL_CPU then
3:
Add current operator to TC
4:
else if CM == USER_DEFINE then
5:
if TD[i] == cpu then
6:
Add current operator to TC
7:
else if TD[i] == npu then
8:
Add current operator to TN
9:
end if
10:
else if CM == DEFAULT then
11:
if OD[i] meets NR then
12:
Add current operator to TN
13:
else
14:
Add current operator to TC
15:
end if
16:
end if
17: end for
18: return TC, TN
2) USER_DEFINE Mode: For advanced users seeking
manual control over task distribution between the CPU and
NPU, the scheduler supports a USER_DEFINE mode. In this
mode, users can specify a binary string for task designation
(denoted as TD), where each bit indicates the assignment of an
operator: “0” for CPU and “1” for NPU. This feature allows
users to experiment with various conﬁgurations, enabling the
evaluation of the hardware platform in acceleration capabilities
and a thorough comparison of performance between the CPU
and NPU.
3) DEFAULT Mode: By default, the scheduler assigns
tasks based on the capabilities of the NPU. It checks whether
each operation meets the NPU’s restrictions. If an operator is
compatible with the NPU, it is added to the NPU task list
(TN). Otherwise, it is assigned to the CPU task list (TC). This
strategy optimizes performance by leveraging the calculation
capacity of NPUs for suitable tasks.
In this mode, the scheduler allows users to modify the
list of “implementable operators” that determines which tasks
can be processed by the NPU. By adjusting these settings,
users can explore the performance impact of speciﬁc classes
of operations on the NPU, while considering any associated
constraints. This level of customization is especially valuable
for researchers and engineers seeking to optimize speciﬁc
computational workloads.
The algorithm begins by iterating over all operators from the
ONNX dialect (OD). Depending on the selected calculation
mode (CM), it applies the appropriate task assignment logic.
Once all operators are processed, the algorithm returns two
lists, TC, representing the tasks allocated to the CPU, and TD,
representing the tasks allocated to the NPU. This structured
and ﬂexible approach allows users to tailor task distribution
based on their speciﬁc needs, whether it involves correctness
veriﬁcation, performance optimization, or in-depth analysis of
computational workﬂows on heterogeneous platforms.
B. Weight Quantizer and Reorderer
The weight quantizer and reorderer are fundamental com-
ponents in the optimization pipeline for deploying CNNs on
NPUs. This process ensures that the weight parameters of
neural networks are efﬁciently formatted and optimized for
the speciﬁc hardware architecture of the NPU, enhancing
performance and reducing computational overhead. On top of
that, if ﬁxed-point computation is to be performed on the CPU,
the weights must also be quantized. The overall process can
be divided into several key stages.
1) Weight Quantization: The ﬁrst step involves the weight
quantizer, which converts ﬂoating-point constant data in the
MLIR dialect into ﬁxed-point data. This conversion applies to
both NPU operators and CPU operators that require ﬁxed-point
computation. The conversion process is guided by dynamic
quantization parameters, which are determined based on the
statistical properties of the data from the upper quantization
platform. Dynamic quantization [37] enables adaptive scaling
of weights, ensuring that the ﬁxed-point representation pre-
serves the necessary precision while minimizing quantization
errors.
2) Integration With NPU Optimized Layer Parameters:
After quantization, the next step is to integrate the quan-
tized data with the layer parameters produced during the
optimization phase of the compiler. This phase introduces
compiler-level loop tiling, which may result in a different
number of layers and associated parameters compared to the
original network. The rearrangement of weights [11] is tailored
to the speciﬁc architecture of the NPU, ensuring that the
weights are organized in a manner that maximizes memory
access efﬁciency and computational throughput.
3) Storing Quantized and Rearranged Weights: After quan-
tization and rearrangement, the processed weight data is
converted back into the MLIR dialect. This step ensures
that the data remains within a standardized and ﬂexible
intermediate representation, which is compatible with the
subsequent stages of the compilation and deployment pipeline.
The quantized and rearranged weights are stored as “NPU
constants,” making it easier to retrieve their memory addresses
during the generation phase of the compilation process.
C. Memory Manager
Heterogeneous architectures that involve both CPUs and
NPUs present complex challenges in memory allocation.
On the software side, CPUs utilize well-established memory
allocation mechanisms, such as the stack, which operates as a
last-in, ﬁrst-out (LIFO) data structure [38]. On the hardware
side, there are two common memory optimization methods,
including the DE and RU mechanisms.
The DE mechanism [39] traverses the overall storage space
of the input FM data and output FM data across all layers, and
then sets the maximum value as the current FM storage space.
This method is particularly effective for networks without
IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMPUTER-AIDED DESIGN OF INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SYSTEMS, VOL. 44, NO. 11, NOVEMBER 2025
(a)
(b)
Fig. 5.
Common NPU memory optimization methods. (a) Bottleneck
of ResNet50 (Stage 1 BTNK 2, original input FM size is 224×224×3);
(b) read-write conﬂicts in DE memory management mechanism; (c) memory
management mechanism for RU.
branches or for architectures utilizing in-place algorithms to
minimize memory usage. However, in certain architectures
with read-write concurrency or in networks with branches,
this approach can lead to read-write conﬂicts. For example,
in Fig. 5(a), the bottleneck (Stage 1 BTNK 2) of Resnet50 is
presented with an original input FM size of 224 ×224 ×3.
Fig. 5(b) illustrates the corresponding DE mechanism. At
the end of the NPU2 calculation, the output FM of NPU1
is released. However, while calculating NPU3, a read-write
conﬂict occurs due to insufﬁcient setup space, resulting in an
error in the result of NPU3.
To address the above issue, we designed a self-check
function to simulate memory allocation and adjust initial
memory sizes upon data conﬂicts. Additionally, we intro-
duced the RU mechanism [Fig. 5(c)], which, unlike the DE
mechanism, avoids precomputing memory size or using self-
checks. It releases memory only after conﬁrming that no
future operations require a speciﬁc layer’s FM. For example,
in Fig. 5(c), NPU1 allocates memory for its output, while
NPU0’s memory remains reserved due to dependencies from
NPU3. Memory is released only after all related computations
are complete, preventing data conﬂicts and ensuring reliable
memory management.
In a CPU/NPU heterogeneous system, memory allocation
can generally be categorized into two types: 1) uniﬁed memory
and 2) discrete memory. Uniﬁed memory allows both the
CPU and NPU to access a shared memory space, simplifying
the programming model by eliminating the need for explicit
data transfers between the CPU and NPU. However, this
model may introduce challenges related to memory bandwidth
and contention, as both processors compete for the same
resources. In contrast, discrete memory allocation assigns

nism, RU_END: end address of the RU mechanism
2: //determine the ofm of the memory to be allocated;
3: for node in network_nodes do
4:
if node.type = SW then

fmSet.insert(node)
7:
end if
8:
else if node.type = HW && node.input = ori_FM then
9:
fmSet.insert(ori_FM)
10:
else if node.type = HW then
11:
fmSet.insert(node)
12:
end if
13: end for
14: //two memory allocation mechanisms (optional);
15: if MECH = RU then
16:
nodes_waddr, RU_END = fmSet.RU(START_HW)
17: else
18:
//self-checking for dual-end mechanism;
19:
DE_END = max(fmSet.ifm + fmSet.ofm)
20:
DE_END = fmSet.self_check(DE_END)
21:
nodes_waddr = fmSet.DE(DE_END, START_HW)
22: end if

24:
if (node.type = SW && prenode.type = HW) ∥(node.type =
HW) then

nodes_raddr = nodes_waddr.ﬁnd(node.ifm)

separate memory spaces for the CPU and NPU. While this
approach can be more complex to manage because it requires
explicit data transfers between the memory of CPU and the
memory of NPU, it can offer more control.
In our work, we chose to use discrete memory allocation.
Algorithm 2 outlines the memory management process of
hardware/software (HW/SW) operators. The memory manager
takes as input a set of nodes and the start address of the
NPU memory (START_HW), and it outputs the read and write
addresses of these nodes. It is noteworthy that only the last
layer of CPU and all NPU operators require memory allocation
(or FM that inputs to NPU directly). This means that all FMs
needed by the NPU, both input and output, are allocated within
the NPU address segment. As a result, the read address of the
ﬁrst operator in the CPU function and the write address of the
last operator are explicitly deﬁned. In the LLVM dialect, we
created a malloc_NPU function to enable the CPU to access
the NPU address segment.
VII. EXPERIMENT AND EVALUATION
To validate the effectiveness of the proposed heterogeneous
compilation framework, a series of experiments are conducted,
focusing on versatility, speed up, and memory efﬁciency. The
experimental setup is as follows.
Software Implementation: Our heterogeneous compilation
framework is based on the ONNX-MLIR toolchain, and the
CAO et al.: HETEROGENEOUS CNN COMPILATION FRAMEWORK
TABLE II
IMPLEMENTATION RESULTS OF THE PROPOSED HETEROGENEOUS COMPILATION FRAMEWORK COMPARED WITH STATE-OF-THE-ART COMPILERS
implementation of heterogeneous expansion, including the
CNN compiler, is carried out using Python 3. The trained
models in the ONNX format and various HW/SW settings are
taken as input. The output is divided into two parts: 1) the
generated heterogeneous instructions as a hex ﬁle and 2) the
original CPU parameters, along with the rearranged network
parameters for NPU, stored as a binary ﬁle in DDR.
Hardware Platforms: The heterogeneous hardware archi-
tecture is composed of a RISC-V CPU and a self-developed
NPU. We choose the RV32IM instruction set for the RISC-V
CPU, which features an 8 KB L1 cache. The NPU is
implemented by SMIC 40 nm CMOS technology, working
at the clock frequency of 200 MHz. It supports commonly
used operations in CNNs, such as convolution, FC, pooling,
etc. To comprehensively evaluate performance, we compare
our results with state-of-the-art studies to measure network
inference latency.
Network Benchmarks: To evaluate the compilation frame-
work across various CNN networks, it is important to cover
different operators and architectures. For depth-based design,
VGG and ResNet are classic models. VGG uses a straight-
forward stack of 3 ×3 convolutions [28], while ResNet
introduces residual connections to address the vanishing gra-
dient problem [31]. RepVGG [40] builds on VGG’s simplicity
with a reparameterization technique that improves infer-
ence efﬁciency while preserving model depth. For multiscale
design, YOLO [41] supports multiscale detection, and its tiny
version is smaller, faster, and ideal for real-time tasks on
resource-limited devices. To further demonstrate the generality
of our framework, we also include a typical deep neural
network (DNN), MNIST, which features the widely-used
Gemm operator.
A. Performance Evaluation
As mainstream frameworks, such as TVM and TensorRT do
not support our heterogeneous hardware or report comparable
metrics, we instead compare our method with representative
works [12], [13], [14], [18] that share similar architectural
assumptions or compilation goals. We note that the reference
works used for comparison do not provide explicit data
on code density, or heterogeneous memory usage, which
limits the scope of metric-level comparison. Therefore, our
evaluation focuses on the implementation structure, and exe-
cution efﬁciency of these frameworks. Table II presents the
implementation results of various CNNs across different archi-
tectures, comparing their performance and resource utilization
metrics. While the table highlights speciﬁc CNNs, such as
YOLOv3-Tiny, YOLOv5s, ResNet50, and MNIST, it is impor-
tant to note that the proposed compilation framework is not
restricted to these models. It is designed to support a wide
range of CNNs, showcasing its general-purpose capability in
handling diverse neural networks.
Our architecture, which is based on the RISC-V RV32IM
CPU and a custom NPU, supports multiple CNNs, as shown
in the results presented in the rightmost column. The NPU is
capable of executing all the operators from these networks,
while the CPU primarily handles instruction recognition and
distribution. This design signiﬁcantly reduces network latency,
resulting in excellent performance. The architecture is ver-
satile, supporting model execution with both high and low
precision (from 32-bit to 8-bit) while maintaining consistent
performance levels. For instance, when deploying YOLOv3
and ResNet50, we achieve competitive performance with
8-bit quantization, allowing for reduced memory footprint
and improved computational efﬁciency without signiﬁcantly
sacriﬁcing accuracy. This ﬂexibility in precision allows the
architecture to adapt to various deployment requirements, from
high-accuracy models, such as ResNet50, to more lightweight,
real-time models, such as YOLOv3-Tiny. It is worth noting
that the operators executed on the CPU maintain a 32-bit
precision to ensure computational accuracy. Therefore, latency
comparisons are made while accounting for any additional
delays introduced by high-precision processing on the CPU,
where applicable.
In comparison to other architectures listed in the table,
such as the extended SODA synthesizer in [14], our archi-
tecture excels in delivering lower memory footprints (e.g.,
24.4 MB for ResNet50 compared to 97.7 MB in [14]) and
reduced latency (e.g., 56.7 ms for ResNet50 compared to
1614.8 ms in [14]) even converted to 32-bit precision. At
the same time, our latency is also 11.2× faster than that

in [18] at the same precision and low frequency. Regarding
YOLOv3-Tiny, while [12] adopts 32-bit numerical precision,
our implementation in 8-bit quantization achieves a latency of
more than one-fourth of theirs. This discrepancy arises because
the accelerator in [12] is speciﬁcally designed for YOLOv3-
Tiny, and our implementation involves data transformation on
the CPU with 32-bit precision. Consequently, the ﬁnal latency
aligns with expectations. Finally, MNIST, as a representative
DNN, is also included in our evaluation. However, the speedup
is not particularly signiﬁcant, as only the Gemm and ReLU
operators can be ofﬂoaded to the NPU for computation.
However, our architecture is capable of supporting the full
range of DNN computations.
It is worth noting that the compilation time in our framework
is on the order of minutes, as it involves multilevel dialect
optimizations and complex ﬁxed-point quantization and rear-
rangement of weights. However, in practical scenarios, this
overhead is negligible since precompilation does not impact
the runtime computational efﬁciency.
B. Task Scheduler Evaluation
In the task scheduling strategy, we offer three modes to
manage the assignment of operations between the CPU and
the NPU based on user-speciﬁc requirements. The efﬁciency
of each mode is veriﬁed as shown in Fig. 6. The experiments
focus on two metrics: 1) code density and 2) inference time.
These metrics are unaffected by memory management because
memory allocation follows a ﬁxed strategy throughout task
scheduling experiments. Hence, the differences observed in
task scheduling conﬁgurations can be attributed solely to
scheduling strategies.
All_CPU Mode: In this mode, we run YOLOv3-Tiny with
an input size of 224 × 224 × 3 (in 8-bit), and observed an
extraordinarily high cycle count of 277 billion cycles. One
reason for this high cycle count may be that the RV32IM ISA
lacks the specialized hardware extensions needed for efﬁcient
deep learning workloads. Many modern architectures leverage
single instruction, multiple data (SIMD), vector operations, or
dedicated accelerators to speed up the matrix multiplications
and convolutions, which are common in deep learning tasks.
Since the RV32IM ISA does not include these features, the
processor likely needs to execute more instructions to achieve
the same results, further contributing to the high cycle count.
Second, a small cache can lead to frequent misses, forcing
the processor to access slower main memory more often.
YOLOv3-Tiny involves many convolutions that repeatedly
access input features, weights, and intermediate results. If the
cache is too small to hold this data across layers, the CPU
spends more time waiting on memory, increasing the cycle
count.
Based on the above analysis, there are two key approaches
for our architecture to enhance the CPU inference speed. First,
we can customize the instruction set for deep learning tasks;
second, a larger cache is needed. Additionally, we tested the
network with an input size of 224 × 256 × 3 to simulate
padding in cols operation mentioned in Section V-B, which
takes around 278 billion cycles, very close to the time taken
without padding. This indicates that the additional overhead
of the CPU to accommodate NPU computation is negligible.
USER_DEFINE Mode: In this mode, we tested how
dividing tasks affects performance, to illustrate the feasibility
of this mode. Fig. 6(a) and (b) provides a detailed comparison
CAO et al.: HETEROGENEOUS CNN COMPILATION FRAMEWORK
of code density and inference time across four different CNN
models. Code density is chosen as a key metric because it
affects storage efﬁciency, power consumption, and execution
performance. Lower code density reduces memory usage,
lowers power by minimizing accesses, and improves efﬁciency
by requiring fewer instructions. Each model is evaluated
under ﬁve scenarios, representing varying proportions of tasks
ofﬂoaded to the NPU, denoted by O_P, ranging from 0 (no
ofﬂoading) to 1 (complete ofﬂoading).
The results indicate a clear trend: as the proportion of tasks
ofﬂoaded to the NPU increases, the code density and inference
time decrease. For each network, the highest code density
and inference time occurs when no tasks are ofﬂoaded (O_P
= 0), with signiﬁcant reductions noted as more tasks are
ofﬂoaded to the NPU. Notably, YOLOv3 exhibits the highest
code density across all tested models, starting from over 30 000
lines in the baseline case. At the same time, due to RISC-V’s
computational inefﬁciency, it also has the highest latency at
O_P = 0, up to 13 234 ms. As the ofﬂoading proportion
increases, there is a signiﬁcant decline in code density and
inference time, highlighting the potential efﬁciency gains.
ResNet50 and ResNet34 exhibit a similar trend, they have
lower overall code densities and inference time compared to
YOLOv3. YOLOv3-Tiny also shows a decrease in code den-
sity and inference time, but the reduction is less pronounced
compared to that of the full YOLOv3 model. This suggests
that larger and more complex networks experience greater
beneﬁts from the tasks ofﬂoading. Overall, this ﬁgure under-
scores the effectiveness of task ofﬂoading in reducing code
density and inference time, which is a critical factor in opti-
mizing computational performance on resource-constrained
platforms.
DEFAULT Mode: In this mode, we evaluated the impact
of ofﬂoading different types of operators to the NPU on the
code density and inference time of various CNN models. As
shown in Fig. 6(c) and (d), 1 × 1 convolutions, 3 × 3 con-
volutions, and convolutions with following element-additions
are ofﬂoaded to the NPU, repectively. ResNet50 and ResNet34
exhibit a similar trend in code density and inference time
reduction. They start with a relatively high code density and
inference time on the CPU, which decreases consistently as
more operations are ofﬂoaded. The most pronounced reduction
occurs when 3 × 3 convolutions are ofﬂoaded, indicating
their computational intensity. This trend is particularly notable
in ResNet34, which contains a greater number of 3 × 3
convolutions than 1 × 1 convolutions. YOLOv3 shows the
highest code density and inference time in the all_CPU mode
but experiences substantial beneﬁts from separately ofﬂoading
both 1 × 1 and 3 × 3 convolutions. YOLOv3-Tiny, as a more
lightly weighted model, also displays a reduction pattern simi-
lar to the larger networks, despite having an overall lower code
density and inference time. Since this model does not have an
element-addition layer, ofﬂoading the Conv+Add operations
is useless. Overall, this analysis underscores the efﬁciency
gains achieved by selectively ofﬂoading speciﬁc types of
operators to the NPU, particularly highlighting the advantages
for in-depth examinations of heterogeneous computational
platforms.

C. Memory Efﬁciency Evaluation
To evaluate the independent impact of memory management
strategies, we conduct all experiments in the ALL_NPU
mode to eliminate interference from task scheduling. We
compare three memory allocation mechanisms implemented
in the NPU: 1) DE; 2) RU; and 3) sequential storage (SS).
Since task scheduling is ﬁxed in this mode, variations in
memory usage reﬂect purely the contribution of memory
management strategies. The experiments aimes to assess the
memory consumption of FMs (the input FM size is 224 ×
224×3) across different mechanisms, with results summarized
in Fig. 7.
Among the three mechanisms, SS consumes the most
memory across all models. For instance, ResNet50 and
YOLOv3 have memory demands of 11.00 MB and 12.51 MB,
respectively, while VGG16 requires 9.99 MB when using SS.
This is because SS stores all intermediate results without any
reuse or optimization. In addition to this, this mechanism leads
to a direct link between memory and model size, with large
gaps in memory between different models. For example, in
the tested models, the minimum FM memory size is 1.14 MB,
while the maximum reaches 12.51 MB.
The RU mechanism shows a notable reduction in memory
usage compared to SS. Speciﬁcally, memory usage for
ResNet50 decreases from 11.00 MB (SS) to 2.41 MB (RU),
while YOLOv3 shows a reduction from 12.51 MB (SS) to
2.84 MB (RU). However, although RU improves efﬁciency, it
still consumes more memory than DE, as it reuses allocated
memory less aggressively in some models.
DE stands out as the most efﬁcient memory allocation
mechanism, reducing memory consumption to minimal levels.
For example, ResNet50 requires only 1.97 MB, achieving a
5.58× reduction compared to SS. The DE mechanism dynam-
ically releases memory that is no longer required for future
operations, leading to a signiﬁcant decrease in FM memory
usage. In addition, the self-checking function discussed in
Section VI-C has been validated in ResNet34 and ResNet50,
as the original memory allocation in the DE mechanism may
not be sufﬁcient for models with branching paths.
Using either DE or RU mechanisms, the difference in FM
memory required between the different networks is much
IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMPUTER-AIDED DESIGN OF INTEGRATED CIRCUITS AND SYSTEMS, VOL. 44, NO. 11, NOVEMBER 2025
smaller, generally under 5 MB. The optimization results for
these two mechanisms show little variation, and in some cases,
such as ResNet34 and YOLOv3-Tiny, their effects are virtually
identical. Therefore, to simplify the compilation process, users
can opt for the more straightforward RU mechanism.
VIII. CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORKS
In this article, we propose a compilation framework based
on RISC-V CPU/NPU co-computing to address the challenge
of mapping and compiling from algorithms to heterogeneous
architectures. By integrating custom operator packing, a ver-
satile task scheduler, and optimized memory management, the
proposed framework signiﬁcantly enhances the efﬁciency and
performance of heterogeneous architectures. Our experimental
results demonstrate that the framework can effectively gen-
erate optimized instructions for various models, signiﬁcantly
enhancing the efﬁciency and performance of heterogeneous
architectures. It achieves up to a 7.06× reduction in code
density and up to a 5.58× improvement in memory usage,
bridging the computational gap between CPUs and NPUs.
Moving forward, we could explore the integration of more
advanced machine learning models, such as transformers, and
their adaptation to more heterogeneous architectures. This will
be part of our future work.
